name: Dwarka
description: Dwarka, also known as Dwaravati, is an ancient city mentioned in Hindu epics and Puranas. It is believed to have been the capital of Lord Krishna's kingdom. According to legend, Krishna established Dwarka after he left Mathura to escape from the relentless attacks of Jarasandha. The city was said to be a magnificent and prosperous metropolis, built on the coast and adorned with palaces, temples, and gardens. It thrived for many years as a center of culture, commerce, and political power under Krishna's rule. Dwarka's significance extends beyond its historical and political importance; it is considered a sacred pilgrimage site for Hindus. The Dwarkadhish Temple, dedicated to Lord Krishna, stands as a testament to the city's enduring spiritual legacy. The city's eventual submersion into the sea after Krishna's departure marks a poignant end to its glorious existence, symbolizing the transient nature of worldly possessions and the cyclical nature of time. Archaeological evidence suggests the existence of an ancient settlement near modern-day Dwarka, lending credence to the mythological accounts. The tales of Dwarka continue to inspire devotion and serve as a reminder of Krishna's divine presence and the lessons he imparted during his time on Earth. Dwarka is a place of great historical, cultural, and religious importance, embodying the ideals of dharma, prosperity, and devotion.
subtitle: Krishna's Golden City, Submerged in Legend

keyMyths.title: Establishment of Dwarka
keyMyths.description: Krishna built Dwarka to escape Jarasandha's wrath, creating a prosperous city for the Yadavas. The city was designed by Vishwakarma, the divine architect.

keyMyths.title: Submersion of Dwarka
keyMyths.description: After Krishna's departure, Dwarka was submerged into the sea due to a curse or the end of the Yuga. This event marked the end of the Yadava dynasty.

sources.author: Vyasa
